one_line_takeaway: By verifying joint (coupled) keypoint deviation instead of per-keypoint deviation via a pruned MILP over a zonotope reachable set, this CMU paper achieves much higher certified robustness rates than a prior decoupled baseline for heatmap-based keypoint detectors (e.g., 99% vs 0% verified under moderate error thresholds) at a cost of slower verification on complex, object-overlapping perturbations.
key_finding: On the paper's own airplane pose-estimation dataset (7320 images, 1920x1200, 23 keypoints; CNN backbone with 5 conv + 5 deconv layers, ~6.57x10^5 parameters, 64x64x3 input), evaluated on 200 seed images with pose-error tolerance parameter alpha in {1.0, 0.5, 0.2, 0.1} scaling rotation bound [10,10,10] deg and translation bound [4,4,20] m: for non-overlapping local-object perturbations with m=1 object, the coupled ('ours') method achieves verified rate 99.5% (199/200) at alpha=1.0 versus the decoupled baseline (Luo et al. 2025) at 47.5% (95/200); at alpha=0.5 ours reaches 99.0% (196/198) while baseline drops to 0.0% (0/198), and the baseline stays at 0.0% for all alpha<=0.5 across every m from 1-4. Empirical (testing-based, 100-sample) verified rate is used as an upper-bound reference, e.g. 100.0% at alpha=1.0 and 88.7% at alpha=0.1 for m=1. For overlapping perturbations (Table 2) both methods' verified rates are consistently lower than non-overlapping, and the coupled method's rate degrades sharply as m (number of local objects) increases (e.g., m=1 alpha=1.0: 95.4% vs baseline 50.0%; m=4 alpha=1.0: 51.6% vs baseline 31.0%), attributed to the airplane occupying a small image fraction so overlapping perturbations directly affect the object more. Verification time (Table 3) for the coupled method is slower than baseline at alpha=1.0 (e.g., m=4: 36.0s vs baseline 21.5s) but faster at alpha=0.1 (m=4: 18.5s vs baseline 39.2s) for non-overlapping images, whereas for overlapping images (Table 5) the coupled method is uniformly slower than the baseline (e.g., m=4, alpha=1.0: 230.8s vs 66.2s). Table 4 shows pruning cuts MILP continuous-variable/constraint counts from ~6.6x10^5 constraints to ~1.9x10^3-1.5x10^4 depending on alpha for non-overlapping images (roughly 3 orders of magnitude), versus about 2 orders of magnitude for overlapping images. Global brightness/contrast perturbation results (Tables 6-9) show similar verified-rate and timing patterns to local non-overlapping occlusions.
core_contribution: The paper proposes the first 'coupled' formal robustness verification framework for heatmap-based keypoint detection networks. Unlike prior work that verifies each keypoint's robustness independently (a decoupled, classification-style check per keypoint), this method verifies the joint deviation of all K keypoints together against a single polytope-shaped specification that captures cross-keypoint interdependence and downstream task requirements (e.g., pose-estimation error bounds translated into joint keypoint-error constraints). Verification is cast as a falsification problem solved with a mixed-integer linear program (MILP) that combines a zonotope over-approximation of the reachable heatmap set (from any existing reachability analysis method) with a Big-M polytope encoding of 'out-of-bound' joint keypoint deviation, plus a 'dynamic indexing' MILP sub-formulation that locates the argmax pixel of each heatmap channel under a variable coordinate offset. If the MILP is infeasible, robustness is certified; if feasible, a counterexample is produced (though feasibility does not prove non-robustness, only that the over-approximated set was insufficient to certify). The paper also introduces a pruning strategy that discards indices provably dominated by other indices' heatmap value bounds, which is shown empirically to shrink MILP size by roughly three orders of magnitude for non-overlapping perturbations and about two orders for overlapping ones.
threat_model: Not an adversarial-attacker threat model in the traditional security sense; the paper's own notion of 'robustness' is verification against a convex hull of input perturbations X (built from a seed image X0 plus locally realistic semantic perturbations or global brightness/contrast shifts), not against an adaptive attacker optimizing a loss. Perturbations studied are: (1) local object occlusions -- one of 40 realistic semantic patches (people, vehicles, up to 150px) placed on/near the target object, categorized as overlapping or non-overlapping with the airplane; (2) global brightness perturbations (pixel value shift b in {1,2}); (3) global contrast perturbations (percentage shift c in {5e-4, 5e-3, 1e-2}). The 'attack' surface is therefore benign-but-worst-case distributional/environmental perturbation robustness (occlusion, lighting, contrast) rather than gradient-based adversarial examples, though the verification machinery (reachable-set + MILP falsification) is the same class of technique used for adversarial robustness certification in the neural-network-verification literature.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation (Conclusion, Section 8) is that under strict output specifications (small alpha) there remains a noticeable gap between the certified/verified robustness rate and the empirical robustness rate obtained via 100-sample testing, which they attribute to conservativeness of the zonotope reachable-set over-approximation. The method is also explicitly incomplete/unsound-in-reverse: MILP feasibility (finding a counterexample in the over-approximated set) does not prove the model is actually non-robust (Remark 2). Verification runtime for the coupled method scales worse than the decoupled baseline as the number of overlapping local perturbation objects (m) grows, and is generally slower under loose specifications (large alpha) for both overlapping and (initially) non-overlapping cases. Evaluation is confined to a single dataset/task (airplane keypoint-based pose estimation with 23 keypoints, a small 64x64 CNN backbone) and a single external reachability-analysis/solver toolbox (ModelVerification.jl); scalability to larger/more complex keypoint detection networks is left as future work, as is developing tighter reachability approximations.

datasets_used: Airplane pose-estimation keypoint dataset from the baseline (Luo et al. 2025): 7320 RGB images, 1920x1200 resolution, 23 keypoints on the airplane surface
200 randomly selected seed images with 4000 generated perturbed images (893 overlapping, 3107 non-overlapping) using 40 realistic semantic occlusion patches (personnel/vehicles, up to 150px)
quantitative_results: Verified rate, non-overlapping local occlusions, m=1, alpha=1.0: ours 99.5% (199/200) vs baseline 47.5% (95/200) vs empirical testing 100.0% (200/200)
Verified rate, non-overlapping, m=1, alpha=0.5: ours 99.0% (196/198) vs baseline 0.0% (0/198)
Verified rate, overlapping local occlusions, m=1, alpha=1.0: ours 95.4% (187/196) vs baseline 50.0% (98/196)
Verified rate, overlapping, m=4, alpha=1.0: ours 51.6% (65/126) vs baseline 31.0% (39/126)
Verification time, non-overlapping, m=4: ours 36.0+/-73.6s at alpha=1.0 vs baseline 21.5+/-12.4s; ours 18.5+/-23.3s at alpha=0.1 vs baseline 39.2+/-14.2s
Verification time, overlapping, m=4, alpha=1.0: ours 230.8+/-190.0s vs baseline 66.2+/-55.8s

MILP size (m=4, non-overlapping, alpha=1.0), continuous variables: 6.61x10^5 without pruning vs 1.95x10^3 with pruning (~3 orders-of-magnitude reduction); binary variables reduced from 9.42x10^4 to 5.31x10^1
baselines_compared: Decoupled per-keypoint classification-style verification baseline of Luo et al. (2025), 'Certifying robustness of learning-based keypoint detection and pose estimation methods' (ACM Trans. Cyber-Physical Systems)

Empirical (testing-based) verified rate computed via 100-sample Monte Carlo evaluation per seed image (method of Tian et al. 2018 / DeepTest), used as an upper-bound reference rather than a competing verifier
